info: Hiragana is one of the three writing systems used in Japanese, primarily for native or Japanese words, grammatical elements, and word endings. It consists of 46 basic characters, each representing a syllable or a combination of a consonant and a vowel sound.

info: Hiragana is one of the three writing systems used in Japanese, primarily for native or Japanese words, grammatical elements, and word endings. It consists of 46 basic characters, each representing a syllable or a combination of a consonant and a vowel sound.
answer: っ (きって kitte) — A small “tsu” that doubles the next consonant. It creates a brief pause before the consonant, like the stop in “bookcase.” In kitte, you hold the t slightly: kit-te, not kee-te.
goal_words: kitte) — A small “tsu” that doubles the next consonant. It creates a brief pause before the consonant,  like the stop in “bookcase.” In kitte,  you hold the t slightly: kit-te,  not kee-te.

info:
Vowel length

Japanese has short vs long vowels, and length changes meaning.

おばさん (obasan) → aunt

おばあさん (obāsan) → grandmother

English speakers often ignore length, which can cause misunderstandings.

Tip: Hold long vowels about twice as long as short ones.
prompt: おばさん - おばあさん ______
answer: おばさん - おばあさん obasan - obāsan
audio_dir: おばさん - おばあさん

info:
Consonant doubling / gemination

The small っ (sokuon) doubles the following consonant.

きって (kitte) → “stamp”

きて (kite) → “come”

English speakers usually skip the pause, which can make the word sound wrong.

Tip: Pause briefly before the doubled consonant.
answer: きって - きて kitte - kite

info:
R vs L confusion

Japanese r is tapped/flapped, not like English R or L.

ら (ra) → between “la” and “ra”

English speakers overdo retroflex R or turn it into L.

Tip: Lightly tap the tongue on the ridge behind your teeth, like a very soft Spanish single R.
answer: らいおん (raion)

info:
N (ん) before consonants

ん adapts to the following sound: n, m, or ng.

日本 (Nihon) → ends like “hone”

English speakers often pronounce it always as a hard n, which can sound unnatural.

Tip: Let it nasalize depending on the next consonant.
prompt: 日本 (Nihon) nasalizes like /hone/ pronouncing hard “n” at the end
answer: 日本 (Nihon) nasalizes like /hone/ pronouncing hard “n” at the end
goal_words: pronouncing hard “n” at the end 日本 (Nihon) nasalizes like /hone/ pronouncing hard “n” at the end

info:
Pitch accent

Japanese is pitch-accented, not stress-accented.

Wrong pitch can make words sound like other words: hashi (bridge) vs hashi (chopsticks)

English speakers naturally stress syllables unevenly, which can obscure meaning.

Tip: Listen carefully and imitate pitch patterns.
answer: はし (橋 - 箸) - HAshi (high-low) - haSHI (low-high)

info:
Mora timing

Every kana represents a mora, a “unit of sound.”

English speakers often reduce unstressed syllables, but Japanese syllables are roughly equal in length.

Tip: Count morae, don’t rush or drop vowels.
answer: さくら (sakura)

info:
Lax consonants

Japanese consonants are lighter than English.

t, d, k, g are not heavily aspirated.

English speakers over-aspirate and make the consonants sound harsher.
answer: たべる (taberu)

info:
English-style diphthongs

Japanese vowels are pure and never glide like English.

えい (ei) → “ay” in they, not “ee-ay”

English speakers tend to turn short vowels into diphthongs unnecessarily.

Tip: Keep each vowel steady and separate.
answer: せんせい (sensei) common mistake - Saying “see-ay” instead of a single long “e”
goal_words: (sensei) common mistake - Saying “see-ay” instead of a single long “e”

answer: おいしい (oishii) common mistake - English-style diphthong “oy” in “boy” instead of separate vowels
goal_words: (oishii) common mistake - English-style diphthong “oy” in “boy” instead of separate vowels

info:
English-style diphthongs

Japanese vowels are pure and never glide like English.

えい (ei) → “ay” in they, not “ee-ay”

English speakers tend to turn short vowels into diphthongs unnecessarily.

Tip: Keep each vowel steady and separate.
answer: すいえい (suiei) common mistake - Gliding to “swee-ay” too quickly
goal_words: (suiei) common mistake - Gliding to “swee-ay” too quickly
